title: TCP/IP Internet Layer
description: Routes packets across networks using IP addresses — the layer that makes the global internet possible.
fullExplanation: The TCP/IP Internet layer maps directly to OSI Layer 3 (Network). It is responsible for logical addressing (IP addresses), routing packets across multiple networks, and delivering packets from source to destination regardless of the underlying physical network. IPv4 and IPv6 are the core protocols. ICMP handles error reporting and diagnostics. IPsec provides encryption at the network layer. Routing protocols (BGP, OSPF) determine the best path.

protocols.name: IPv4
protocols.description: 32-bit addressing, 4.3 billion addresses, most internet traffic today

protocols.name: ICMP
protocols.description: Error reporting and diagnostics — ping, traceroute

protocols.name: ICMPv6
protocols.description: IPv6 error reporting + Neighbor Discovery (replaces ARP)

protocols.name: IPsec
protocols.description: Network-layer encryption and authentication — used in VPNs

protocols.name: IGMP
protocols.description: Internet Group Management Protocol — IPv4 multicast group membership

protocols.name: BGP
protocols.description: Border Gateway Protocol — routes between autonomous systems (internet backbone)

protocols.name: OSPF
protocols.description: Open Shortest Path First — routes within an organization

keyFacts: IP is connectionless and unreliable — no delivery guarantee (Transport layer handles that)
Every packet routed independently — may take different paths
TTL (IPv4) / Hop Limit (IPv6): prevents infinite routing loops
Fragmentation: IPv4 routers can fragment; IPv6 only source host fragments
Internet layer PDU is called a 'Packet'
NAT operates at this layer — translates private IPs to public IPs
